rationale: The aerial after-photo shows the brick building at 1 Bailey Ave completely surrounded by floodwater with inundation reaching at least the base of the first-floor windows, indicating water intrusion sufficient to damage first-floor drywall, electrical outlets, mechanical systems (water heater, furnace), and lower cabinetry — consistent with a Moderate (DS2) damage state; the before photos show no pre-existing flood damage or elevated mechanical systems.
caveats: Assessment based solely on one oblique aerial after-photo with no ground-level post-flood imagery; water depth estimate is approximate from aerial perspective without surveyed grade elevations; interior damage cannot be confirmed from exterior aerials alone; building may have flood mitigation measures not visible in available imagery.

rationale: No after-flood photos available for this address.
caveats: after-photo folder is empty; nothing to assess

rationale: The after photos show workers with ladders near the entrance and what appears to be active remediation/repair work underway at the first-floor level, consistent with moderate flood damage; the sign on the building has been updated (from 'Land Use Review Board' to 'Natural Resources Board'), suggesting post-flood operational changes, and the first-floor exterior shows subtle discoloration and darkening on the lower siding and foundation relative to the before photos, indicative of water intrusion reaching into the first-floor interior. Baldwin Street is in a low-lying area of Montpelier known to have experienced 2–3 ft of inundation during the July 2023 flood.
caveats: Exterior-only assessment; after photos are relatively similar in angle to before photos but image resolution limits detection of fine-grained water staining or tide marks; no interior photos available; workers and ladders suggest active remediation but scope of interior damage cannot be directly confirmed from these views alone.

rationale: Aerial after-photos show floodwater completely surrounding the building with water visibly reaching approximately mid-first-floor wall height (estimated 4–5 ft above grade at the building exterior), submerging the front entry portico base and ground-level features that are clearly dry and intact in the before photos; at this depth, complete inundation of first-floor interiors including all mechanical, electrical, drywall, cabinetry, and flooring is expected, and the water level approaching lower second-floor windows on at least one elevation suggests potential damage extending into upper-story non-structural components, consistent with a damage state of 3.
caveats: Assessment is based solely on aerial oblique imagery during active flooding; no post-recession ground-level exterior or interior photos are available to confirm actual water-line marks or structural damage; aerial angle limits precise water depth calibration; no visible structural collapse or wall failure is observed, so damage state 4 is not indicated from exterior evidence alone.

rationale: The after photo shows the red single-story building nearly submerged, with floodwater visibly reaching approximately mid-wall height — well above the door threshold and approaching the roofline eaves — indicating water depth of roughly 4–5 ft at the building exterior; compared to the before photo showing a clean, intact single-story red structure with standard door and window heights, this level of inundation would result in complete first-floor interior destruction including drywall, electrical, mechanicals, and contents, consistent with Damage State 3.
caveats: Assessment based on a single blurry, low-resolution after photo taken during active flooding from an oblique aerial angle; exact water depth is estimated by comparing visible wall exposure to known building height from before photo; no post-flood recession photos available to confirm final waterline marks or structural integrity; interior damage not directly observable.

rationale: The aerial after-photos show the Capitol Plaza / 100 State St complex surrounded by floodwater that appears to reach approximately 3–4 feet above grade at the building exterior, submerging the ground-floor storefronts (Central Vermont Adult Basic Education, Signature Styles, Northfield Savings Bank) up to or above their window sills; comparing to the before photos, these ground-floor commercial spaces had finished interiors, large glazed storefronts, and mechanical equipment at grade level, all of which would be inundated, consistent with moderate damage including destruction of first-floor contents, base electrical, water heater/HVAC, and lower cabinetry with likely significant mold.
caveats: Assessment is from oblique aerial video stills only with no post-flood ground-level or interior photos; water depth estimate is approximate based on comparison to known building features; upper floors appear structurally intact so damage is likely confined to ground floor and basement systems; actual interior damage may be higher or lower than estimated from exterior only.

rationale: The after photo shows the building (identifiable as the pink/mauve rear facade matching the pre-flood Street View rear elevation) surrounded by rapidly moving brown floodwater reaching approximately to the top of the first-floor window sills or just above — roughly 4–6 feet above grade — which would inundate the entire first floor interior well above mid-wall height, destroying drywall, electrical, mechanicals, and all first-floor contents, and potentially reaching lower second-floor levels given the visible water depth. No structural collapse is evident, but the depth of inundation visible in the after photo is consistent with Damage State 3 (extensive non-structural damage throughout the first floor, mid-wall electrical destroyed, major mold and sewer backup expected).
caveats: After photo is a low-resolution aerial/elevated video frame taken during active flooding, making precise water depth and structural damage difficult to assess; no post-flood recession photos available to confirm actual water mark height on the building; assessment is exterior-only with no interior access; angle is oblique and image quality is poor.

rationale: The active-flood photo shows water at approximately 1.5 ft depth at the building exterior, reaching near the bottom of the arched storefront windows; the interior photo confirms floodwater and thick mud/sediment entered the first-floor commercial space, covering the tile floor and reaching lower cabinets, consistent with moderate damage — first-floor flooring, lower cabinetry, and contents are damaged, though wall drywall above baseboard level and upper cabinets appear largely intact in the visible interior shot.
caveats: No formal BEFORE reference photos were provided for direct comparison; interior view is limited to one angle and may not capture full extent of drywall or mechanical equipment damage; assessment is exterior and partial interior only; the building appears to be a single-story commercial brick structure so upper-story damage is not applicable.

rationale: The after aerial photos show the building (Vermont History Museum / Pavilion Building at 109 State St) surrounded by floodwater with street-level inundation visibly reaching approximately 2–3 feet at the exterior; the adjacent street is deeply flooded and water appears to be at or just above the first-floor threshold level, consistent with damage to first-floor mechanical systems, lower cabinets, flooring, and drywall, while the upper stories and structural elements appear intact and undamaged compared to pre-flood reference photos.
caveats: Assessment is from oblique aerial imagery only with low resolution; exact water ingress height into the building interior cannot be confirmed from exterior alone; trees partially obscure the base of the front facade; no ground-level post-flood photos available to confirm interior water line on walls.

rationale: The after photos show the building exterior in largely the same condition as the before photos — white clapboard siding intact, windows undamaged, no visible waterline staining, no debris deposition, and no structural distortion compared to the pre-flood baseline; the landscaping appears intact and there is no visible evidence of inundation reaching the first-floor living space. However, the after photos may have been taken after cleanup and the building sits in a low-lying area of Montpelier that experienced significant flooding, so interior damage cannot be ruled out from exterior inspection alone.
caveats: Exterior-only assessment; after photos appear to be taken post-cleanup with no active floodwater visible, making waterline detection difficult; the before and after photo sets appear to show two different but adjacent structures (the Greek Revival at the front and a larger multi-unit building behind), complicating direct comparison; no visible tide marks or debris lines on siding; interior damage cannot be assessed from these images.

rationale: The aerial/during-flood photo clearly shows floodwater surrounding the building at what appears to be approximately 2–3 feet above grade, consistent with water reaching the first-floor interior; the post-flood street-level photo shows the brick structure and upper stories are structurally intact with no visible collapse or displacement, but the first-floor entry portico area appears darkened and potentially damaged compared to the clean pre-flood baseline, suggesting moderate interior damage to first-floor systems (water heater, furnace, lower electrical, flooring) without drywall damage extending to upper floors.
caveats: Assessment is limited to exterior views only; the aerial during-flood photo is low resolution making precise water depth estimation uncertain; first-floor interior condition cannot be confirmed without interior access; the post-flood street-level photo is partially obscured by vegetation and vehicle; the 110 State St building is a masonry civic/office structure, which may respond differently to inundation than a typical residential building.

rationale: The aerial during-flood photo shows floodwater surrounding the building at approximately ground-floor/first-floor arcade level, consistent with roughly 2 feet of water at the exterior; post-flood ground-level photos show the brick facade and upper stories structurally intact with no visible waterline staining on the exterior brick above the arcade base, but the ground-floor commercial space (M&T Bank) with large glazed arched windows would have experienced water intrusion sufficient to damage first-floor finishes, electrical outlets, HVAC, and commercial fixtures — consistent with a moderate (DS2) damage state.
caveats: Assessment is exterior-only with no interior access; the during-flood aerial is oblique and low-resolution making precise water depth difficult to determine; post-flood street-level photos appear to be taken after cleanup/restoration may have already begun (fall foliage timing); no visible tide/waterline mark on exterior brick confirmed, so interior damage depth is inferred from flood context and building position rather than direct evidence.

rationale: The aerial after-photo shows the Vermont State Capitol grounds with what appears to be shallow surface water or saturated ground on the pathways and lower lawn areas immediately surrounding the building, while the Capitol structure itself and its elevated front steps appear largely above the flood level; the surrounding streets to the west show significant inundation but the Capitol's elevated site on the State House lawn appears to have limited direct water intrusion, suggesting at most minor water contact at foundation/basement level consistent with Damage State 1.
caveats: Assessment is based solely on a single oblique aerial photo taken during the flood event; no ground-level after photos are available to confirm actual water depth at the building foundation; the Capitol sits on elevated ground relative to Main Street, making precise depth estimation difficult from this angle; interior damage, basement/crawlspace conditions, and rear/side elevations cannot be assessed; the aerial perspective makes it difficult to distinguish between wet pavement and actual standing water at the building perimeter.

rationale: The aerial during-flood photo shows floodwater surrounding the building's perimeter at what appears to be grade level or slightly above — consistent with water reaching the first-floor slab or lower wall of this elevated masonry structure; the post-flood exterior photos show no visible structural damage to the brick or masonry, windows appear intact, but the building's first-floor interior (including mechanical systems, lower cabinets, and flooring) would be expected to have sustained moderate damage given the observed inundation depth and the known extent of the 2023 Montpelier flood in this area along State Street.
caveats: Assessment is exterior-only with no interior access; the aerial photo provides the clearest flood-stage evidence but resolution is limited; the post-flood ground-level photos appear to be taken weeks or months later after some cleanup, obscuring waterline evidence on the masonry; the building sits on elevated stone steps on the front facade which may have partially mitigated first-floor inundation on that side; no clear tide marks visible on exterior walls in available photos.

rationale: Comparing before and after photos, the building exterior shows no visible flood waterlines, staining, or structural damage on the siding, foundation, or porch elements; the after photos show the building in notably better cosmetic condition than the before photos (which showed scaffolding and roof damage under active renovation), suggesting any flood impact was minor at most. A dumpster/lift vehicle is present in one after photo which may indicate interior remediation work, but no exterior evidence of significant inundation height is visible on the yellow clapboard siding.
caveats: Assessment is exterior-only with no interior access; the before photos show the building mid-renovation with pre-existing damage, making flood-specific damage differentiation difficult; no clear tideline or waterline staining is visible on the exterior; the presence of construction equipment in the after photos could reflect flood remediation or continuation of pre-existing renovation work; after photos are oblique and do not show all facades equally.

rationale: The aerial after-photo shows extensive standing floodwater surrounding the building with water depth estimated at approximately 3–4 feet at grade level, consistent with water entering the first floor of this large marble-clad government building (Vermont State Office Building); the post-flood ground-level after-photos show no visible tide/mud lines or structural damage on the exterior marble facade, but the flood depth observed in the aerial view is sufficient to cause moderate interior damage including damage to first-floor mechanical systems, electrical outlets, drywall, and furnishings, while the upper floors appear unaffected.
caveats: No close-up post-flood ground-level photos showing waterline marks on the exterior are available; damage assessment relies heavily on aerial flood extent photo; interior damage cannot be confirmed from exterior photos only; the building sits on elevated ground with entrance steps which may have partially mitigated inundation depth at the first floor threshold.

rationale: The after photo shows an aerial view with floodwater completely inundating the surrounding area to a depth estimated at 4–5 feet at grade, with water visibly reaching well above the ground floor level of the low-profile utility/mechanical building seen in the before photos; the before photo shows a single-story gray masonry mechanical/utility structure with rooftop equipment, and the flood water appears to be at or near roof level of the lower wing, indicating complete first-floor inundation and likely destruction of all mechanical, electrical, and interior components throughout.
caveats: After photo is an oblique aerial view with low resolution, making precise water depth and wall-line water height difficult to confirm; the before photo shows only a partial rear/side view of what appears to be a utility/mechanical annex building, not the full primary structure at 122 State St; no ground-level post-flood photos are available; building identity match between before and after photos is approximate given the aerial perspective.

rationale: The aerial after photo clearly shows floodwater surrounding and inundating 126 State St to an estimated 2–3 ft depth at the exterior, sufficient to cause partial first-floor drywall damage, loss of base electrical, water heater, and furnace damage consistent with DS2; the post-flood ground-level photos show no structural collapse or major exterior damage but the inundation depth is corroborated by the aerial context relative to the surrounding flood extent.
caveats: Ground-level after photos appear to be taken after water receded and show no visible waterline marks at this resolution; water depth estimated from aerial imagery which is oblique and approximate; interior damage not directly observable; no close-up post-flood ground photos showing tide marks or first-floor damage details.

rationale: The aerial after-photo shows floodwater inundating the State Street corridor in downtown Montpelier, with water levels appearing to reach approximately 2 feet at street grade in the vicinity of 128 State Street; the ground-level post-flood photo of the building shows no visible structural damage to the brick exterior, roof, or upper floors, but the flood depth in the area is consistent with water entering the first floor, likely damaging flooring, baseboards, lower electrical outlets, HVAC equipment, and lower cabinets, placing this at Moderate (DS2). Compared to the before photos, the structure itself appears intact with no collapse or major exterior deterioration.
caveats: Assessment is primarily based on aerial flood depth estimation; no close-up post-flood ground-level photos show a water stain line or direct interior damage evidence; building sits on elevated stone foundation steps which may have partially mitigated interior inundation depth; interior damage cannot be confirmed from exterior photos alone.

rationale: The after photos show a window AC unit installed in a first-floor window of the ell addition (not present in before photos), consistent with post-flood replacement of a damaged HVAC system, and what appears to be a tideline/discoloration at the base of the porch skirt and foundation approximately 2–3 feet above grade; the building's structural envelope and upper stories appear intact with no visible collapse or major exterior damage, suggesting moderate flood intrusion into the first floor.
caveats: After photos are exterior only, taken at dusk/low light reducing detail visibility; no clear high-water mark is definitively confirmed from this angle; interior damage cannot be assessed; the building sits on a sloped lot which complicates water depth estimation; only front and partial side facades are visible in after photos.

rationale: The aerial after-photo clearly shows floodwater surrounding 132 State St at a depth consistent with mid-first-floor inundation (estimated ~2–3 ft at grade), with brown turbid water reaching at least partway up the foundation and first-floor level; the ground-level after-photos show no visible high-water marks or structural damage on the exterior cladding, but the aerial context confirms significant first-floor flood intrusion consistent with damage to flooring, lower drywall, base electrical, water heater, and furnace. No structural collapse or upper-floor water marks are evident.
caveats: Exterior-only assessment; ground-level after-photos appear to have been taken after water receded and show no obvious tide/mud lines due to image resolution and lighting; water depth is estimated from aerial photo context relative to known building dimensions; interior damage cannot be confirmed from exterior photos alone; the building sits at a slightly elevated grade on State St which may have reduced actual interior inundation depth compared to surrounding streets.

rationale: The aerial after-photos show widespread floodwater surrounding 133 State St (the large white/granite multi-story state office building at Governor Aiken Ave), with water clearly inundating the ground-level perimeter and surrounding streets to an estimated depth of 2-4 feet at the building exterior; the before photos show clean granite facades with no pre-existing water damage, and the after photos indicate water reached at least the first-floor level of this elevated building, suggesting damage to basement/lower-level mechanical systems, first-floor interiors, and base electrical components consistent with a moderate (DS2) assessment.
caveats: Aerial oblique angle only — no close-up ground-level after photos of this specific building's facades are available; water depth estimate is approximate based on comparisons with known street and vegetation heights in aerial imagery; interior damage cannot be directly confirmed from exterior aerials; the building sits slightly elevated from street grade which may have reduced interior inundation relative to street water depth.
